nativeRange.habitat: Hobby cultivar of the Bucephalandra genus, which is endemic to the island of Borneo, where wild populations grow as rheophytes on rocks and submerged wood along fast-flowing rainforest streams

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood or other porous hardscape for the rhizome to attach to
shaded or moderately lit midground placement, often used to fill gaps between hardscape
low-to-moderate flow across the rhizome to mimic rheophyte stream conditions
stable, established tank — generally advised against introducing into newly cycled systems

goodWith: Neocaridina and Caridina shrimp, which graze biofilm on the leaves
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern, Bolbitis and other Bucephalandra cultivars
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers such as large cichlids that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters where buce melt is more likely

summary: Bucephalandra Theia is a slow-growing epiphytic plant from Borneo, named after the Greek titaness. Its broad, rippled leaves display a shifting iridescent sheen in deep green, blue, purple, and reddish-brown. Care mirrors the genus: attach the rhizome to rock or driftwood and never bury it.
careNotes:
Bucephalandra Theia (Bucephalandra sp. 'Theia') is a hobby cultivar of the Borneo-endemic Bucephalandra genus, named after the Greek titaness. In the wild, Bucephalandra species grow as rheophytes — plants that anchor themselves to rocks and submerged wood along fast-flowing rainforest streams rather than rooting in soil — and Theia carries those same habits into the aquarium. It is grown as an epiphyte, meaning it is attached to hardscape such as driftwood, lava rock, or smooth stone and kept well away from substrate; burying the rhizome (the thick horizontal stem from which the leaves emerge) causes it to rot. Care is broadly similar to Anubias, the other common epiphytic plant in the freshwater hobby.

Theia is noted for its soft, broad, rippled leaves, which develop a pronounced iridescent sheen that shifts between deep green, blue, purple, and dark olive to reddish-brown depending on lighting conditions. Mature foliage tends to settle into deeper earthy tones over time. It is typically placed in the midground and is well suited to filling gaps between hardscape pieces, providing a visual contrast to fine-leaved or grassy species. Low to moderate lighting is sufficient; stronger LEDs are reported to intensify the iridescent effect. CO2 supplementation is not required but is generally considered beneficial for growth. Light fertilization through the water column is commonly reported to support coloration and overall health.

Theia is a slow grower. New collectors should also be aware of 'buce melt', a common setback in which leaves soften and die back when the plant is first moved from the emersed (above-water) conditions of a nursery into a submersed aquarium, or after sudden shifts in water parameters or lighting. Recovery from a firm, healthy rhizome is generally reported over the following weeks, provided water conditions remain stable. Keeping Theia in a well-established aquarium with steady parameters reduces the likelihood of a prolonged melt.
breedingNotes: Standard hobby propagation is by rhizome division: cut the rhizome at a natural bend with clean, sharp scissors, leaving each piece with several leaves and visible roots, and reattach the new division to a separate piece of hardscape using super glue gel, sewing thread or fishing line. Growth is slow, so a freshly divided plant typically needs several weeks to begin putting out new leaves.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirms Borneo origin (fast-moving streams), temperature 70-82°F (≈21-28 °C), low-to-moderate lighting, slow growth, foreground-to-midground placement, broad rippled leaves with deep green/blue/purple iridescent hues, epiphytic attachment to driftwood or rocks, requirement not to bury the rhizome (rot risk), and pairing with Anubias, Java fern and Bolbitis.

additional.name: Buceplant
additional.notes: Secondary source. Cross-checked Borneo endemism, pH 6.0-7.5, low-to-medium lighting (intensified iridescence under stronger LEDs), CO2 not necessary but recommended, slow growth, midground placement (suited to filling gaps between hardscape), easy care level, propagation by rhizome division with sharp scissors, epiphytic attachment to rocks or driftwood, do-not-bury-rhizome rule, and the cultivar-specific traits of soft broad leaves, pronounced iridescent sheen, and dark olive to reddish-brown coloration.

additional.name: Bucephalandra (genus parent entry fw-plant-016)
additional.notes: Cross-referenced shared genus husbandry: water parameters (21-28 °C, pH 6.0-7.5, gH 3-15), low-to-medium lighting, optional CO2, slow growth, epiphytic placement, do-not-bury-rhizome rule, buce melt during emersed-to-submersed transition, and propagation by rhizome division. Used to fill the temperature range (absent on Buceplant) and pH range (absent on Modern Aquarium).
